docs.title: ResQConnect Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the ResQConnect technical documentation. This site provides detailed information about the system architecture, data models, API endpoints, and workflows.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: ResQConnect is a disaster management platform designed to connect affected individuals with responders and resources efficiently.

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
• Backend Architecture: High-level overview of the backend system.
  • Data Models: Entity-Relationship diagrams and schema definitions.
  • Sequence Diagrams: Visual representation of key system workflows.
  • API Reference: Detailed API endpoint documentation.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text: By default, the API is accessible at http://localhost:8000.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
All secured endpoints require a valid Firebase ID Token passed in the Authorization header.

Header Format: Authorization: Bearer <firebase_id_token>

docs.title: POST /requests
docs.text:
Create a new help request.

Payload:

{
  "search_text": "I need help with food",
  "name": "John Doe",
  "contact": "+1234567890",
  "location": {
    "lat": 12.34,
    "lng": 56.78
  },
  "type": "food",
  "urgency": "high"
}

Response:

  • 201 Created: Returns the created Request object.

docs.title: GET /requests
docs.text:
List all requests (requires admin/responder role).

Response:

  • 200 OK: Returns a list of Request objects.

docs.title: GET /requests/{req_id}
docs.text:
Get details of a specific request.

Response:

  • 200 OK: Returns the Request object.
  • 404 Not Found: If the request does not exist.

docs.title: POST /disasters
docs.text:
Create a new disaster event (Admin only).

Payload:

{
  "name": "Flood in City X",
  "description": "Severe flooding...",
  "location": {
    "lat": 12.34,
    "lng": 56.78
  }
}

Response:

  • 201 Created: Returns the created Disaster object.

docs.title: GET /disasters
docs.text:
List all active disasters.

Response:

  • 200 OK: Returns a list of Disaster objects.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The ResQConnect backend is built using FastAPI (Python 3.10+) and follows a microservice-like architecture. It leverages Google Firebase (Firestore) for data persistence and authentication, and Celery with Redis for asynchronous task processing (primarily for AI agent workflows).

docs.title: High-Level Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    Client[Frontend / Mobile App] -->|HTTP/REST| API[FastAPI Backend]

    subgraph "Backend Services"
        API -->|Auth/Data| Firestore[(Google Firestore)]
        API -->|Task Queue| Redis[(Redis Broker)]
        Redis -->|Consume| Worker[Celery Worker]
        Worker -->|Update| Firestore
    end

    subgraph "External Integrations"
        Worker -->|LLM Calls| OpenAI[OpenAI API]
        Worker -->|Notifications| FCM[Firebase Cloud Messaging]
    end

docs.title: 1. API Layer (FastAPI)
docs.text:
• Role: Handles HTTP requests, authenticates users, and validates input using Pydantic schemas.
  • Location: app/api/
  • Key Modules:
    • auth.py: User authentication and role management.
    • requests.py: CRUD operations for help requests.
    • disaster.py: Management of disaster events.

docs.title: 2. Data Layer (Firestore)
docs.text:
• Role: NoSQL Schema-less database for storing Users, Requests, Tasks, and Resources.
  • Location: app/core/firebase.py (Connection logic), app/crud/ (Data access logic).
  • Pattern: Repository pattern via CRUD modules.

docs.title: 3. Asynchronous Task Queue (Celery)
docs.text:
• Role: Handles long-running or background tasks, such as:
    • AI Agent workflows (matching requests to resources).
    • Generating summaries or reports.
  • Location: app/celery_config.py, app/worker.py (implied).

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
backend/
├── app/
│   ├── api/            # Route handlers
│   ├── core/           # Configuration & security
│   ├── crud/           # Database operations
│   ├── schemas/        # Pydantic models (Data validation)
│   ├── services/       # Business logic (if separated)
│   └── main.py         # Application entry point
├── tests/              # Pytest suite
└── requirements.txt    # Python dependencies

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The backend uses Pydantic models for data validation and schema definition, which map to Firestore documents.

docs.title: Entity Relationship Diagram
docs.text:
erDiagram    
    User ||--|{ Request : "created_by"
    User ||--|{ Resource : "owned_by"
    Disaster ||--|{ Request : "associated_with"
    Request ||--o| Task : "assigned_task_id"
    Task }|--|{ Resource : "requires"

    User {
        string uid PK
        string email
        string display_name
        string role_id
        GeoPoint location
        timestamp created_at
    }

    Request {
        string id PK
        string created_by FK
        string disaster_id FK
        string type_of_need
        string description
        string status
        string assigned_task_id FK
        GeoPoint location
        timestamp created_at
    }

    Disaster {
        string id PK
        string name
        string description
        string type
        GeoPoint location
        timestamp created_at
    }

    Task {
        string id PK
        string request_id FK
        string status
        string assigned_agent_id
        timestamp created_at
    }

    Resource {
        string id PK
        string name
        string type
        string status
        string owner_id FK
        GeoPoint location
    }

docs.title: User
docs.text: Field Type Description uid String Unique identifier (Firebase Auth UID). email String User's email address. role_id String Role identifier (e.g., admin, responder, affected_individual). location GeoPoint Current location of the user (lat, lng).

docs.title: Request
docs.text: Field Type Description id String Unique identifier for the request. disaster_id String ID of the disaster event this request is related to. type_of_need String Type of help needed (e.g., food, medical). status String Status of the request (open, in_progress, fulfilled, closed). location GeoPoint Location where help is needed.

docs.title: Disaster
docs.text: Field Type Description id String Unique identifier for the disaster. name String Name of the disaster event. location GeoPoint Epicenter or primary location of the disaster.

docs.title: 1. Create Help Request Workflow
docs.text:
This diagram illustrates the flow when a user submits a new help request. The request is stored in Firestore, and an asynchronous task is triggered via Celery to process the request (e.g., matching it with available resources).

sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant API as FastAPI Backend
    participant DB as Firestore
    participant Q as Redis Queue
    participant W as Celery Worker

    U->>API: POST /requests (payload)
    API->>API: Validate Token (Auth)
    API->>DB: Save Request (status="open")
    DB-->>API: Request ID
    API->>Q: Enqueue Agent Task (request_id)
    API-->>U: Return Created Request

    Q->>W: Process Task
    W->>W: Analyze Request (AI/Logic)
    W->>DB: Update Request (e.g., assign resource)

docs.title: 2. Resource Assignment Workflow (Simplifed)
docs.text:
This diagram shows how a resource is assigned to a request.

sequenceDiagram
    participant W as Celery Worker (Agent)
    participant DB as Firestore
    participant N as Notification Service

    Note over W: Triggered by new Request or Resource availability
    W->>DB: Query Open Requests & Available Resources
    W->>W: Match Logic (Location, Type, etc.)

    alt Match Found
        W->>DB: Update Request (status="assigned")
        W->>DB: Update Resource (status="busy")
        W->>N: Notify User & Responder
    else No Match
        W->>DB: Log "No match found" (Retry later)
    end
